against_electronic_derailleur: MGU reduces exposed drivetrain maintenance more than electronic derailleur systems, while still offering automatic/semi-automatic shift potential.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: MGU centralizes mass and keeps rear wheel simpler than hub gear/CVT alternatives, with stronger integrated diagnostics potential.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Ordinary mid-drive lacks low-maintenance transmission differentiation.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM can sell low-maintenance, belt-friendly, wide-range, premium integrated experience with strong user-visible value.
why_oem_might_not: Cost, weight, supplier lock-in and service training remain risks.

against_electronic_derailleur: Electronic derailleur still leaves chain/cassette/derailleur exposed under high load and frequent stop-start use.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: Hub gear/CVT solves much of the low-maintenance need, but puts complexity and mass in rear wheel; torque/load limits must be checked.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Ordinary mid-drive still depends on external transmission durability.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM can position MGU around loaded starts, low maintenance, centralized mass and family/commercial reliability.
why_oem_might_not: Need high-load lifespan proof, service strategy and total vehicle cost control.

against_electronic_derailleur: Electronic derailleur is serviceable but exposed; long-distance riders may value sealed systems more.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: Rohloff/Pinion gearbox already strong substitutes; MGU must add motor-shift integration and centralization.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Ordinary mid-drive lacks drivetrain durability story.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM can sell long-range, low-maintenance, sealed drivetrain and wide-ratio reliability.
why_oem_might_not: Market size and willingness to accept MGU weight/cost need validation.

against_electronic_derailleur: Electronic derailleur systems are light, familiar and sport-accepted; SRAM/Shimano already solve some shift-control pain.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: Hub gear/CVT less common for aggressive eMTB due rear mass/feel concerns; MGU centralizes mass and removes rear derailleur.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Ordinary high-performance mid-drives are strong and lighter; MGU must add real climbing/impact/maintenance advantage.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM can create flagship differentiation, simplify rear end and show technical leadership.
why_oem_might_not: Weight, NVH, manual control, impact durability and rider trust are high barriers.
preliminary_rank: strategic validation, not safest first market

against_electronic_derailleur: Electronic shifting/ordinary drivetrains may be enough for many users; cost sensitivity is higher.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: enviolo Automatic and similar hubs already solve automatic low-maintenance commuting well.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Ordinary mid-drives or hub motors may be cheaper and sufficient.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM can use MGU for premium commuter differentiation and low-maintenance belt builds.
why_oem_might_not: MGU cost may exceed user willingness to pay; substitutes are mature.

against_electronic_derailleur: Electronic derailleur is lighter and accepted; user maintenance tolerance is higher.
against_hub_gear_or_cvt: Hub systems are also weight/feel challenged in this segment.
against_ordinary_mid_drive: Lightweight mid-drives like TQ/FAZUA-style benchmarks likely better match segment expectations, though not verified in current evidence.
why_oem_would_choose_mgu: OEM may choose MGU only for niche concept differentiation.
why_oem_might_not: Weight and integration complexity likely overpower pain-point benefits.
